coredata.dc:title: Ferric citrate corroding nickel foam to synthesize carbon quantum dots@nickel–iron layered double hydroxide microspheres for efficient water oxidation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Zhang, Yuzhen
Zhou, Huajun

Yuan, Kai

Gao, Hui
Wang, Yanzhong
coredata.dc:description: The design of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalysts with high catalytic efficiency and durability is of great significance for promoting hydrogen production via water electrolysis. Here, a one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots@nickel–iron layered double hydroxide (CQDs@NiFe-LDH) composites based on corrosion engineering. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) effectively modulates the electronic structure and charge distribution of nickel–iron layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH), resulting in high oxygen evolution reaction with an overpotential of 257 mV at 100 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope of 38.73 mV dec−1. Furthermore, CQDs@NiFe-LDH can be operated continuously for 300 and 100 h without the significant performance degradation at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH and seawater solutions, respectively, indicating high catalytic stability. The excellent OER capabilities of CQDs@NiFe-LDH is attributed to the fact that CQDs can not only modulate the electronic structure of NiFe-LDH but also facilitate the transfer of protons between intermediates during the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), thereby enhancing the material’s intrinsic catalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots
Layered double hydroxides
Oxygen evolution reaction
Corrosion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is funded by the following projects: Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (20210302123049), Key R&D Projects in Shanxi Province (202102090301024, 202102090301025, 202202030201003 and 202202090301022), Shanxi Innovation Center for the Co-processing and Utilization of Industrial Solid Waste (202104010911016), Special Project of Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchange of Shanxi Province (No. 202204041101003), Youth Project of Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2022QE108), Key R & D Projects of Shanxi Province (202302030201002) and National Defense Fundamental Research Initiative (JCKY2023408C012). The authors wish to extend their gratitude to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for providing assistance with TEM, Raman, and XPS analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–b) SEM images of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 at different magnifications, (c) histogram of particle size distribution of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3, (d–f) TEM images of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 at different magnifications, (g) Selected Area Electron Diffraction Diagram, (h) HRTEM images, (i–j) the lattice fringe image after inverse Fourier transform corresponding to the lattice fringe (three times of test were performed on the above calculations, and the error range of the data was retained to three digit after the decimal point.), (k-o) EDX electron energy spectrum.

(a) XRD images of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3, CQDs@NiFe-LDH and NiFe-LDH, (b) Fourier transform infrared images of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and NiFe-LDH, (c) Raman images of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and NiFe-LDH, (d) Local enlarged Raman image.
(a) XPS gross spectral images of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and NiFe-LDH, (b–d) high resolution XPS of C 1s, Ni 2p and Fe 2p in CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and NiFe-LDH.

(a) Comparison of LSV curves of different catalytic materials, (b) the overpotential comparison of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3, CQDs@NiFe-LDH and NiFe-LDH at different current densities (the calculation error of the overpotential value is calculated three times.), (c) the Tafel slope curves were calculated according to the LSV curves, (d) Cdl curves were calculated from CV curves, (e) TOF values corresponding at 1.50 V, (f) Nyquist plots (the calculation error of the TOF value is calculated 3 times, and the error range of the data was retained to two digit after the decimal point.), (g) comparison of the catalytic properties of the prepared catalytic materials with those of recently published LDH materials, (h) stability test curves of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 at a current density of 100 mA cm−2.
(a-b) Comparison of high-resolution XPS electron spectra of Ni and Fe elements before and after CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 stability test, (c) In-situ Raman characterization of CQDs @ NiFe-LDH-0.3.

(a–b) LSV curves of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and NiFe-LDH at different pH conditions; (c-d) LSV curves of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and NiFe-LDH at 1.5 V vs. RHE voltage corresponding to the current density versus pH (the calculation error of the Stop value is calculated 3 times, and the error range of the data was retained to two digit after the decimal point.).

(a) LSV curve of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 in simulated seawater solution, (b) Comparison of overpotential under different test conditions (the calculation error of the overpotential value is calculated three times.), (c) comparison of LSV curves of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 in real seawater solution, (d) stability test of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 in seawater solution, (e) LSV curve of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 after seawater stability test.

(a) LSV curves of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3//Pt/C and RuO2//Pt/C overall water splitting test, (b) the stability test of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3//Pt/C total water splitting, and (c) LSV curves of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3//Pt/C post 100 h of durability testing. (no iR compensation was performed for total water splitting).

head.ce:title: Ferric citrate corroding nickel foam to synthesize carbon quantum dots@nickel–iron layered double hydroxide microspheres for efficient water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Efficient Hydrogen Storage & Production Technology and Application, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Efficient Hydrogen Storage & Production Technology and Application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalysts with high catalytic efficiency and durability is of great significance for promoting hydrogen production via water electrolysis. Here, a one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots@nickel–iron layered double hydroxide (CQDs@NiFe-LDH) composites based on corrosion engineering. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) effectively modulates the electronic structure and charge distribution of nickel–iron layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH), resulting in high oxygen evolution reaction with an overpotential of 257 mV at 100 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope of 38.73 mV dec−1. Furthermore, CQDs@NiFe-LDH can be operated continuously for 300 and 100 h without the significant performance degradation at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH and seawater solutions, respectively, indicating high catalytic stability. The excellent OER capabilities of CQDs@NiFe-LDH is attributed to the fact that CQDs can not only modulate the electronic structure of NiFe-LDH but also facilitate the transfer of protons between intermediates during the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), thereby enhancing the material’s intrinsic catalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive exploitation of fossil fuels has caused resource depletion and severe ecological problems, prompting the advancement of sustainable energy [1]. Among various renewable resources, hydrogen energy is considered an ideal substitute for fossil fuels due to its environmentally friendly nature and high energy density [2]. Among the different methods for producing hydrogen, the production of high purity hydrogen by water electrolysis is a simple and feasible method [3]. However, the anodic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) during water electrolysis impedes the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) due to its complex four-electron transfer process [4]. Currently, precious metal oxides (RuO2, IrO2) are still effective catalysts for the OER. Nevertheless, the limited availability of ruthenium and iridium makes their extensive industrial use impractical. [5]. On the other hand, electrolyzing fresh water to produce hydrogen is likely to exacerbate water scarcity. Therefore, a viable alternative to using fresh water for electrolysis is to use seawater, which is abundant on Earth. However, owing to the high concentration of chloride ions in seawater, a competitive chlorine oxidation reaction (ClOR) will be induced at the anode at high current densities when the OER performance of electrocatalysts is inadequate [6,7]. In order to achieve the complete electrolysis of seawater, it is necessary to prepare non-precious metal-based catalytic materials with excellent OER catalytic properties [8].

In recent years, transition metal oxides, sulfides and phosphides [9–11] have been widely studied as non-precious metal-based OER catalysts. Transition metal layered double hydroxide (TM-LDH) has particularly garnered attention due to its low cost and simple preparation. NiFe-LDH has been extensively studied due to its high catalytic activity in an alkaline environment [12,13]. Among various methods for synthesizing layered double hydroxide (LDH), the corrosion of metal foam by metal salt is considered to be an efficient method to prepare self-supported LDH with high catalytic efficiency and strong stability [14,15]. Since the etching process creates a rough surface on the metal foam substrate, it facilitates the tight contact between LDH and metal substrate, which can further improve the electron transfer rate of LDH. For instance, Huang et al. [16] reported that the self-supported NiFe-LDH with the excellent OER catalytic performance was achieved by etching nickel foam substrates with potassium ferrate under hydrothermal conditions. It achieved 100 mA cm−2 current density at an overpotential of only 303 mV, and can catalyze stably for 100 h. Nevertheless, the catalytic activity of NiFe-LDH is still not meeting expectations. To enhance the OER performance, many strategies such as surface modification, interface engineering and morphology modulation have been developed [17,18]. Among them, the construction of heterogeneous interfaces by introducing a second phase is a straightforward and efficient approach to increase the catalytic properties of LDH.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received increasing attention due to their low cost, high electrical conductivity, and rich surface functional groups [19]. It is of great significance that CQDs with functional groups (such as –COOH and –NH2) and multi-component heteroatom doping (including N, S, P, and B) can enhance the catalytic performance of composites by facilitating electron transfer through intrinsic interactions. [20,21]. Moreover, CQDs can effectively modulate the material's geometric structure and crystal phase of materials to disclose more active sites. [22]. Therefore, CQDs has become a potential candidate for enhancing the electrocatalytic efficacy of LDH electrocatalysts. For example, Ding et al [23]. synthesized N-CDs using ammonium citrate via a hydrothermal method and then hybriding with NiFe-LDH to prepare N-CDs/NiFe-LDH/NF composite materials. The formed MNC bonds in NiFe-LDH promotes the electron transfer mechanism and enhances the inherent activity of NiFe-LDH, leading in elevated catalytic activity in 1.0 M KOH and alkaline seawater. However, the common approach is usually first to synthesize CQDs via high-temperature hydrothermal reactions, and then hybriding with LDHs, which inevitably results in unnecessary energy loss and wastes the time and cost required for material synthesis.

Herein, we successfully prepared CQDs@NiFe-LDH composite electrocatalysts through a single-step hydrothermal approach involving etching nickel foam with ferric citrate. Thanks to the modulated morphology and electronic structure of NiFe-LDH with CQDs, the as-prepared CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 displays an overpotential of just 257 mV at 100 mA cm−2, and a low Tafel slope of 38.73 mV dec−1. It can stably operate for 200 h without the significant degradation. In addition, the –COOH group on the surface of CQDs is conductive to assisting the deprotonation process and enhancing the proton transfer during the OER process [24], which further enhances the OER catalytic performance of NiFe-LDH. Moreover, CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 can maintain high catalytic activity in seawater electrolysis and work stably for 100 h at 100 mA cm−2. This paper presents a novel method to prepare highly efficient self-supporting OER catalysts with corrosion engineering and composite.

Ferric citrate (FeC6H5O7, 99.9 %, AR), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %, AR), iron nitrate (Fe(NO3)3, 98.5 %, AR), were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Potassium hydroxide (KOH, 99 %, AR), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5 %, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent. Ethanol without water content was sourced from Tianjin Fuyu Co., Ltd. Bohai Sea water was gathered from the China region. Nickel foam (NF) was acquired from Suzhou KeShengHe Co., Ltd., with a pore size of 110 ppm and a thickness of 1.5 mm, which was ultrasonically treated in 3 M HCl solution, deionized water and anhydrous ethanol for 30 min, 5 min and 5 min, respectively, and was then used directly without drying treatment.

First, 1 mmol of ferric citrate was dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water and absolute ethanol mixed solution with a 1:1 volumetric ratio, and then a certain amount of citric acid (X = 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 mmol) was dissolved in the above solution. The cleaned NF was placed in the mixed solution and transferred to a reactor with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner, and then heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The products were repeatedly washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol, followed by drying in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for a period of 6 h, named as CQDs@NiFe-LDH-X (X = 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 mmol). For comparison, the samples were prepared under identical experimental parameters with only citric acid ferric and iron nitrate, which were designated as CQDs@NiFe-LDH and NiFe-LDH, respectively.
The structural characterization and electrochemical measurement are described in the supplementary materials.

The synthetic process of self-supporting CQDs@NiFe-LDH and NiFe-LDH catalysts via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method is illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, ferric citrate dissociates into citrate ions and Fe3+. The strongly oxidizing Fe3+ ions can corrode the NF substrate to release Ni2+ and Fe2+ ions. During the hydrolysis process, an alkaline environment will be formed, thereby forming NiFe-LDH. At the same time, citrate ions will polymerize and carbonize to form carbon quantum dots at high temperature and pressure during the hydrothermal treatment. [25] Meanwhile, the formed CQDs would be simultaneously loaded into NiFe-LDH to form the CQDs@NiFe-LDH composite. The chemical equations corresponding to these transformations are as follows:
(1) FeC6H5O7 + H2O → (C6H5O7)3− + Fe3+
(2) Fe3+ + Ni → Fe2+ + Ni2+
(3) Fe2+ + O2 + H2O → FeOOH + OH–
(4) Fe3+ + Ni2+ + OH– →NiFe-LDH ↓

To ensure the corrosion of NF by Fe3+ ions, a small amount of citric acid was added to suppress the hydrolysis of Fe3+ ions during the synthesis process. The photographs of samples (Fig. S1) show different colors, indicating different formation mechanisms. When ferric nitrate is used for corrosion, Fe3+ can directly react with NF as shown in equation (2). As the generated Fe2+ is further oxidized into Fe3+ ions by dissolved oxygen, OH– is generated. The local alkaline environment will lead to the co-precipitation of Ni2+ and Fe3+ to form NiFe-LDH.

The morphologies of the synthesized materials were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The surface of the blank nickel foam (NF) was observed to be relatively flat (Fig. S2a-b). And as depicted in Fig. 2 a–c, CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 exhibits a spherical structure with a diameter of 1.5–3 μm, and is uniformly coated on the surface of NF. In contrast, when iron nitrate is used as corrosion agent to etch NF, the formed NiFe-LDH exhibits nanosheet structure (Fig. S2c–d), indicating that iron salts as corrosion agents have a considerable effect on the morphology of LDH. In addition, the effect of the amount of ferric citrate (FC) on the morphology of samples was systematically investigated. As shown in Fig. S3a–b, when the FC content is 0.5 mmol, the NF surface is covered with a large number of irregular nanoparticles. With the increase of FC to 1 mmol, interconnected honeycomb structures composed of nanosheets are formed (Fig. S3c–d). When the FC content is up to 1.5 mmol, the coexistence of spheres and nanosheets is observed (Fig. S3e–f). However, when the FC dosage further increases up to 2 mmol, scattered irregular particles are observed on the NF surface (Fig. S3g–h). In order to further optimize the morphology of CQDs@NiFe-LDH, the effect of citric acid contents at a fixed FC content of 1 mmol has been investigated. When the CA content is 0.1 mmol, spherical particles are formed and dispersed between the aggregated nanosheets (Fig. S4a–b). With the increase of CA contents to 0.3–0.5 mmol, the SEM images (Fig. S4c–f) indicate that the formed regular spheres are densely coated on the NF surface. The TEM image (Fig. 2d) of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 further confirms the spherical structure, and the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows that CQDs with a diameter of approximately 2 nm are uniformly dispersed on the surface of NiFe-LDH (Fig. 2e–f). The selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern is illustrated in Fig. 2g, in which no obvious diffraction spots are observed, but three relatively wide Laue rings are displayed, indicating the poor crystallinity of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 [26]. In addition, the corresponding spacing can be echoed with the (012) (018) and (116) planes of NiFe-LDH, respectively. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2h) reveals the lattice fringes of CQDs and NiFe-LDH. After the inverse Fourier transform (IFRT), the lattice fringes and the corresponding intensity distribution are shown in Fig. 2i-j, respectively. The interplanar spacing is 0.234 and 0.251 nm, respectively, which can be attributed to the (101) plane of CQDs [27,28] and the (012) plane of NiFe-LDH [29], respectively. The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental analysis mapping in Fig. 2k–o shows that the C, O, Ni and Fe elements are uniformly distributed in CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3.

The phase compositions and crystal structure of samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 3 a. The strong peaks at 44.5°, 51.8°, and 76.4° correspond to the (111), (200) and (220) planes of NF, respectively (PDF#040850) [30]. For the XRD pattern of NiFe-LDH, the characteristic peaks at 11.5°, 23.3° and 34.6° are attributed to the (003), (006) and (012) planes of NiFe-LDH (PDF#51-0643) [31]. In contrast, no distinct characteristic peaks were observed for CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and CQDs@NiFe-LDH, suggesting the poor crystallinity of NiFe-LDH, which is in good agreement with the results of the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2g). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy were employed to analyze the surface functional groups and bonding energies of samples. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the peaks at 1383 and 1562 cm−1 of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 are assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric oscillations of –COOH groups on the surface of CQDs [32], which were not observed in NiFe-LDH. Additionally, the Raman spectra (Fig. 3c) exhibit strong peaks at 1580 and 1410 cm−1, corresponding to the G peak and –COOH groups in carbon materials [33,34]. The magnified portion of the Raman spectra in the range of 300–700 cm−1 is illustrated in Fig. 3d. For CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3, the peak at 498 cm−1 is assigned to the vibrations of Ni-O bonds in NiFe-LDH [35], while the peaks at 560 and 679 cm−1 is attributed to FeOOH [36–38], indicating that the introduction of CQD is favorable for promoting the generation of high-valent Fe species. Therefore, FeOOH components with high OER catalytic activity can be easily formed in CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3. In contrast, for NiFe-LDH, the peaks were only observed at 445 and 498 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrations of Ni-O bonds [39]. The above analysis illustrates that CQDs can modulate the morphology and electronic structure of NiFe-LDH, thereby enhancing the OER performance.

In order to further analyze the valence changes of the elements and the effect of CQDs on the electronic configuration of NiFe-LDH, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4 a) indicate the occurrence of Ni, Fe, O and C, which is in accordance with the EDX results. As shown in Fig. 4b, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s in CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 exhibits the main peaks at 284.8, 286.7 and 288.6 eV corresponding to CC, C–OH and CO peaks, respectively, which is similar to NiFe-LDH [40]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ni 2p (Fig. 4c) show two main peaks located at 856.1 and 873.8 eV, corresponding to Ni2+ 2p3/2 and Ni2+ 2p1/2, respectively, along with two satellite peaks at 862.0 and 877.1 eV (denoted as Sat.) [41,42]. In comparison with NiFe-LDH, the peak of Ni 2p3/2 in CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 shifts to a higher binding energy by approximately 0.5 eV. Similarly, the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 4d) of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 exhibits a similar phenomenon. The peaks of Fe3+ 2p3/2 and Fe3+ 2p1/2 located at 711.1 and 724.4 eV shift by 0.4 eV compared with those of NiFe-LDH [43,44]. The results suggest that the electronic correlations between CQDs and NiFe-LDH promote the generation of high valence Ni and Fe ions in NiFe-LDH, resulting in high OER performance.

The electrochemical performance of catalysts was tested in a 1 M KOH solution by using a typical three-electrode system at room temperature. The LSV curves with 95 % iR compensation (Fig. 5 a) show that CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 demonstrates superior OER catalytic performance compared with CQDs@NiFe-LDH, NiFe-LDH and RuO2. The distribution of overpotential values at different current densities (Fig. 5b) illustrates that the overpotentials of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 are only 210, 248 and 257 mV at current densities of 10, 50 and 100 mA cm−2, respectively, which are much lower than those of CQDs@NiFe-LDH (η10 = 237, η50 = 269 and η100 = 288 mV) and NiFe-LDH (η10 = 271, η50 = 313 and η100 = 335 mV). To determine the optimal OER catalytic performance of catalysts, the effect of FC and CA contents on the OER performance are investigated. As shown in Fig. S5a, the catalytic performance of CQDs@NiFe-LDH is optimal when the FC amount is 1 mmol. With the addition of CA, the catalytic performance of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-X first increased and then decreased. When the CA amount is 0.3 mmol, it exhibits the best OER performance (Fig. S5b). These results indicate that the contents of FC and CA have an impact on the OER effectiveness of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-X.

To further analyze the OER catalytic kinetics, the Tafel slopes of electrocatalyst are calculated from the LSV curves. As shown in Fig. 5c, CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 has the smallest Tafel slope of 38.73 mV dec−1, which is much lower than those of CQDs@NiFe-LDH (47.27 mV dec−1) and NiFe-LDH (92.28 mV dec−1), suggesting the fast catalytic reaction rate of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3. The double-layer capacitance (C dl) value is an effective parameter for evaluating the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of the catalyst. The Cdl values were computed by using cyclic voltammetry (CV) at different scan rates within the non-Faradaic region (Fig. S6a–c). As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the Cdl value of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 is 2.34 mF cm−2, which exceeds the values of CQDs@NiFe-LDH (2.32 mF cm−2) and NiFe-LDH (0.45 mF cm−2), indicating the abundant active sites of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3. The normalized LSV curves with ECSA (Fig. S7) show that CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 maintains high current densities across the entire voltage range, indicating its superior intrinsic catalytic activity. Furthermore, the turnover frequency (TOF) values of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3, CQDs@NiFe-LDH and NiFe-LDH were calculated to further investigate the inherent catalytic effectiveness of electrocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 5e, the TOF value of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 is 7.37 s−1, which is 3.6 times more than that of CQDs@NiFe-LDH (2.04 s−1) and 24.5 times more than that of NiFe-LDH (0.33 s−1), respectively, suggesting the superior inherent catalytic activity of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3. Fig. 5f displays the electrochemical impedance spectra of catalysts. The semicircle in the high-frequency portion represents the electron-transfer resistance (Rct ) of catalysts, which correlates with the electrocatalytic kinetics. It is evident that CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 exhibit the smallest Rct value of 0.36 Ω, which is notably lower than those of CQDs@NiFe-LDH (0.55 Ω), NiFe-LDH (1.32 Ω) and NF (58.4 Ω). It suggests that CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 has a fast electron transfer rate and reaction kinetics. The above catalytic performance of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 suggests that CQDs can effectively enhance the electrical conductivity of NiFe-LDH, promote the electron transfer rate, and further enhance the intrinsic catalytic activity. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5g, CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 demonstrates superior OER performance compared with the recently reported LDH-based electrocatalyst. Additionally, the stability of the catalyst is also a crucial factor affecting its practical application capability. Fig. 5h shows that CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 can operate steadily for 300 h at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 without significant degradation. And the LSV curve (Fig. S8) indicates that it still maintains the high OER catalytic activity after the stability test.

The morphology, crystal structure and valance state of electrocatalyst after the stability test were characterized by SEM, XPS and TEM. As shown in Fig. S9a–b, the SEM images show that the spherical structure of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 collapsed and transformed into nanoparticles coated on the NF surface. The EDS spectra (Fig. S9c–d) show that the Ni and Fe elements are still uniformly distributed on the surface of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3. The high-intensity XPS spectra of Ni, Fe and O after the stability test are shown in Fig. 6a–b and S10. The binding energies of both the Ni 2p3/2 and Fe 2p3/2 peaks shifted to high binding energy levels, suggesting the formation of high oxidation valence after the OER reaction. In Fig. S10, it can also be clearly seen that the M−OH bond shifted to a low binding energy, and the peak area corresponding to the M−O bond increases, indicating the generation of high-valence NiFeOOH with high catalytic activity in the OER process. [45,46] The TEM image (Fig. S11) shows that the crystallinity of the electrocatalyst decreases after the stability test, and the lattice spacing of 0.230 nm corresponding to CQDs can still be observed, indicating the stabile combination with NiFe-LDH [35,46]. In order to investigated the surface reconstruction of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 during the OER process, the in-situ Raman spectra were performed in the range of potentials from open circuit potential (OCP) to 1.7 V (vs. RHE) with the potential gradient of 0.1 V) in 1 M KOH. The Raman spectra (Fig. 6 c) showed two characteristic peaks at near 430 and 498 cm−1 at OCP, which were attributed to the tensile vibration of the Ni2+-O and the Ni-O stretching mode of NiOOH [36]. With the increase of applied potential (≥0.1 V vs. RHE). the NiOOH peak shifted to a higher wave number with a range of about 10 cm−1, indicating that there is a strong electronic interaction between CQDs and NiFe-LDH. When the potential increased up to 1.2 V, the peak at ∼ 670 cm−1 was observed, which corresponds to FeOOH species [47], indicating the generation of FeOOH during the OER process. When the applied voltage further increased up to 1.7 V, the characteristic peaks of NiOOH and FeOOH become lower. In fact, due to the superiority of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 in OER electrocatalytic performance, a large number of oxygen bubbles are generated on the surface of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 at high voltage during the test, so a small amount of active MOOH may be stripped from the electrode. Therefore, the surface reconstruction to form NiOOH and FeOOH are the real active sites of OER.

In the natural optical system II (PS-II), there is a redox reaction that accelerates proton transfer. The key to this process is the oxygen evolution center (OEC), which releases oxygen as a complete reaction process. The functional groups on the amino group of the protein (carboxyl and imidazole ligand skeleton) promote proton transfer during water oxidation. [33,48] The role of –COO– group in proton transfer in the OER process has been discussed in some recent studies on electrolyzed water oxidation [49]. In view of the large number of –COO– groups on the surface of CQDs, the pH dependence of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and NiFe-LDH in different alkaline environments was tested to further understand the origin of the enhanced reaction dynamics of CQDs@NiFe-LDH (Fig. 7 a–b). As illustrated in Fig. 7c–d, the current density corresponding to different pH conditions at 1.5 V (vs RHE) was collected. The slope of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 is 0.18 (decades pH−1), which is 8.5 times lower than the slope of NiFe-LDH (1.53, decades pH−1). The low slope indicates that the OER kinetics of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 can be ignored for pH dependence, indicating that abundant –COO– groups can promote the proton transfer [50]. –COOH can help the active substance capture protons in *OH and *OOH intermediates during the OER process, thereby accelerating proton transfer and promoting the release of O2. Since the external alkaline environment can help the carboxylic acid to deprotonate, the protonation/deprotonation forms a cycle [24]. The above analysis shows that CQDs not only regulates the electronic structure of NiFe-LDH, but also can promote the proton transfer, further improving the OER catalytic performance of catalysts.

With the aim of expanding the practical implementation of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3, the OER activity of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 was evaluated in artificial seawater and alkaline marine environments. As shown in Fig. 8 a–b, the LSV curves of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 change slightly, and the difference in overpotentials is small at the current density of 50 and 100 mA cm−2. In the real seawater electrolyte solution (Fig. 8c), the OER performance decayed slightly, but it can still maintain a low overpotential of 283 mV at 100 mA cm−2, which is significantly less than the 480 mV of overpotential demanded to trigger the ClOR reaction [51]. In addition, the complex catalytic environment of various ions and microorganisms in the seawater environment may affect the activity and stability of catalysts. Nevertheless, CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 exhibits no obvious decay for 100 h, (Fig. 8d). The LSV curve (Fig. 8e) only slightly decreases after the stability test, and an obvious current oxidation peak can be observed at the potential of 1.3–1.5 V due to the transformation of Ni2+ to Ni3+ [52]. The SEM image (Fig. S12) shows the similar morphology evolution to that of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 in 1 M KOH, indicating that it is a normal morphology transformation phenomenon rather than being poisoned by Cl− in seawater. The high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S13) also show that the corresponding Ni 2p and Fe 2p peaks have moved to elevated binding energy after the stability test, indicating the formation of high-valence oxides (hydroxides) acting as actual active sites for the OER.

On account of the high OER catalytic activity of CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3, CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3 and Pt/C were used as the anode and cathode, respectively, to assemble a CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3//Pt/C water electrolysis cell. As indicated in Fig. 9 a, the assembled CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3//Pt/C cell can reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at a low voltage of 1.48 V. In addition, Fig. 9b shows that the CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3//Pt/C can continuously operate and remain stable for 100 h at a large current density of 100 mA cm−2. The electrochemical performance of water electrolysis cell decreased slightly after the stability test (Fig. 9c), indicating that the CQDs@NiFe-LDH-0.3//Pt/C system has very high stability and has potential value for practical industrial applications.

We have successfully prepared a self-supporting CQDs@NiFe-LDH catalyst via a simple one-pot hydrothermal approach. Thanks to the electronic interaction of NiFe-LDH and CQDs and the proton transfer effect of the –COOH group, the OER catalytic performance of CQDs@NiFe-LDH was effectively enhanced. Compared with the traditional CQD@LDH composite synthesis method [53,54], this method is simple and easy to mass production. Moreover, this proton transfer promotion mechanism provides a new idea for understanding and designing efficient OER catalysts. The prepared CQDs@ NiFe-LDH-0.3 has an overpotential of only 257 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, and has a long-term stability of more than 300 h. In addition, it can effectively resist the corrosion and toxicity of chloride anions and pollutants in real seawater electrolysis. In addition, a low voltage of 1.48 V can reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in the assembled total water electrolysis cell. Therefore, this paper proposes a simple method to synthesize CQDs to enhance the intrinsic catalytic activity of NiFe-LDH.

Yongping Qu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Resources. Yuzhen Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Huajun Zhou: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Peihua Zhao: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Kai Yuan: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Rui Zhou: Visualization, Supervision, Resources. Hui Gao: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Yanzhong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The authors wish to extend their gratitude to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for providing assistance with TEM, Raman, and XPS analyses.
